flashcards.back: A function f: A → B is onto if for every element b in the codomain B, there exists at least one element a in domain A such that f(a) = b. In other words, range(f) = B.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between onto and one-one functions?
flashcards.back: One-one (injective) maps distinct domain elements to distinct codomain elements. Onto (surjective) ensures every codomain element has a preimage. A function can be both (bijective), either, or neither.

flashcards.front: If |A| = m and |B| = n, when does an onto function exist?
flashcards.back: An onto function from A to B exists only if m ≥ n. The domain must have at least as many elements as the codomain.

flashcards.front: State the condition for composition of onto functions to be onto
flashcards.back: If f: A → B and g: B → C are both onto functions, then g∘f: A → C is also onto. However, if g∘f is onto, it does not necessarily mean g is onto.
A bijective function is both one-one (injective) and onto (surjective). It creates a perfect pairing between domain and codomain elements.

flashcards.front: How many onto functions from set A to set B when |A| = n and |B| = m (n ≥ m)?
flashcards.back: Number of onto functions = m! × S(n,m), where S(n,m) is the Stirling number of the second kind (number of ways to partition n objects into m non-empty subsets).

flashcards.front: What is the inverse of an onto function?
flashcards.back: An onto function f: A → B has a right inverse g: B → A such that f(g(b)) = b for all b ∈ B. If f is bijective, the inverse is unique.

flashcards.front: Define codomain and range of a function
flashcards.back: Codomain is the set of possible output values specified when defining the function. Range (or image) is the actual set of outputs produced by the function. For onto: Range = Codomain.

flashcards.front: What is a constant function? Is it onto?
flashcards.back: A constant function maps all elements in domain to a single element in codomain. It is NOT onto unless the codomain has exactly one element.

flashcards.front: Prove: If f: A → B and g: B → C are onto, then g∘f is onto
flashcards.back: Proof: Let c ∈ C. Since g is onto, ∃b ∈ B with g(b)=c. Since f is onto, ∃a ∈ A with f(a)=b. Then (g∘f)(a)=g(f(a))=g(b)=c. Hence g∘f is onto.

flashcards.front: What is the pigeonhole principle relation to onto functions?
flashcards.back: Pigeonhole principle states: If n items are put into m containers and n > m, some container has more than one item. For onto functions: domain size must be ≥ codomain size (n ≥ m).

flashcards.front: Can a finite set have an onto function to a larger set?
flashcards.back: No. An onto function from A to B requires |A| ≥ |B|. If |A| < |B|, it's impossible for every element in B to have a preimage.
To test if f is onto: (1) Write down the codomain B, (2) For each element b ∈ B, solve f(a) = b for a, (3) If every b has at least one solution a, f is onto.

flashcards.front: Define right inverse of a function
flashcards.back: A right inverse g of f: A → B is a function g: B → A such that f(g(b)) = b for all b ∈ B. A function has a right inverse if and only if it is onto.

flashcards.front: Give an example of an onto function from R to [0,∞)
flashcards.back: f(x) = x² is onto from R to [0,∞) because for any y ≥ 0, there exists x = √y such that f(x) = y.
